title: Add `glift` command for creating gate-level information flow tracking models and optimization problems
body:
Overview:
----
This PR adds the `glift` command, used to create gate-level information flow tracking (GLIFT) models by the ["constructive mapping" approach](http://kastner.ucsd.edu/wp-content/uploads/2013/08/admin/todaes14-glift_lattice.pdf) (see figure 2 and section 2.5).  It also provides an option to instead [set up a 2QBF-SAT optimization problem](https://www.epfl.ch/labs/lap/wp-content/uploads/2018/05/HuNov16_ImpreciseSecurityQualityAndComplexityTradeoffsForHardwareInformationFlowTracking_ICCAD16.pdf) (see Section 4.2 and the 'precise' column in Table 2) that can be solved to yield a smaller yet functionally equivalent GLIFT model.

Background:
----
Typically, the GLIFT models produced by this command are used with a SAT solver or test bench suite and simulator or hardware emulator to determine if some forbidden information flow(s) can occur.  However, the GLIFT logic overhead for the most precise taint tracking--which, just to be clear, does *not* mean that the GLIFT model is completely precise, as the constructive mapping approach effectively guarantees false-positive information flows--comes in at around 5x the amount of logic as the circuit being modeled.

This can be prohibitively expensive, and typically leads to GLIFT users dramatically reducing the precision of the information flow tracking.  Unfortunately, this results in an explosion of false-positive information flows that must be manually ruled out.  This scalability problem greatly reduces the utility of GLIFT modeling.

Therefore, any technique that can reduce the size of these models without introducing additional imprecision (or minimizing the amount of imprecision added for a given amount of area reduction) is highly desirable, and it can be worth spending a long time optimizing the GLIFT model.

Example:
----
The authors of [the ICCAD16 paper above](https://www.epfl.ch/labs/lap/wp-content/uploads/2018/05/HuNov16_ImpreciseSecurityQualityAndComplexityTradeoffsForHardwareInformationFlowTracking_ICCAD16.pdf) have graciously provided some of their experiments for this PR.  See the Verilog files and example GLIFT optimization scripts in `examples/smtbmc/glift`.  However, be warned that these problems take quite a long time to optimize on powerful hardware, so do not expect to be able to do much on your laptop.
 
For a simple, straightforward example, see `examples/smtbmc/glift/mux2.ys`:
```
read_verilog <<EOT
module mux2(a, b, s, y);
        input a, b, s;
        output y;

        wire s_n = ~s;
        wire t0 = s & a;
        wire t1 = s_n & b;
        assign y = t0 | t1;
endmodule
EOT
techmap
copy mux2 spec
copy mux2 uut
copy mux2 solved
delete mux2
glift -create-precise-model spec
glift -create-instrumented-model uut
glift -create-instrumented-model -no-cost-model solved
design -push-copy
miter -equiv spec uut qbfmiter
flatten
delete spec uut solved
qbfsat -assume-outputs -assume-negative-polarity -write-solution mux2.soln qbfmiter
design -pop
qbfsat -specialize-from-file mux2.soln solved
opt
miter -equiv spec solved proofmiter
flatten proofmiter
sat -prove trigger 0 proofmiter
delete proofmiter
stat solved spec
```

This also has some `logger -expect` lines and can be used as a test.  It is not included in the `tests/` directory, however, because it relies on a separate installed solver, which I believe the CI system lacks.

When run, this gives the relevant output:
```
1. Executing Verilog-2005 frontend: <<EOT

[...]

7. Executing QBFSAT pass (solving QBF-SAT problems in the circuit).
Replaced input \in_b_t with $allconst cell.
Replaced input \in_a_t with $allconst cell.
Replaced input \in_s_t with $allconst cell.
Replaced input \in_a with $allconst cell.
Replaced input \in_b with $allconst cell.
Replaced input \in_s with $allconst cell.
Adding $assume cell for output(s): "\trigger"
Minimizing wire "\gate.__glift_weight".

[...]

7.3. Solving QBF-SAT problem.
Launching "/home/user/github/yosys/yosys-smtbmc -s yices -t 1 -g --binary /tmp/yosys-z3-FJV8oN/problem1.smt2 2>&1".
Solver finished in 0.068 seconds.
Problem is satisfiable with \gate.__glift_weight = 15.

[...]

7.15. Solving QBF-SAT problem.
Launching "/home/user/github/yosys/yosys-smtbmc -s yices -t 1 -g --binary /tmp/yosys-z3-FJV8oN/problem5.smt2 2>&1".
Solver finished in 0.068 seconds.
Problem is NOT satisfiable with \gate.__glift_weight <= 10.
Wire \gate.__glift_weight is minimized at 11.

                  /$$$$$$      /$$$$$$$$     /$$$$$$$
                 /$$__  $$    | $$_____/    | $$__  $$
                | $$  \ $$    | $$          | $$  \ $$
                | $$  | $$    | $$$$$       | $$  | $$
                | $$  | $$    | $$__/       | $$  | $$
                | $$/$$ $$    | $$          | $$  | $$
                |  $$$$$$/ /$$| $$$$$$$$ /$$| $$$$$$$//$$
                 \____ $$$|__/|________/|__/|_______/|__/
                       \__/


[...]

10. Executing MITER pass (creating miter circuit).
Creating miter cell "proofmiter" with gold cell "spec" and gate cell "solved".

[...]

12. Executing SAT pass (solving SAT problems in the circuit).

[...]

SAT proof finished - no model found: SUCCESS!

                  /$$$$$$      /$$$$$$$$     /$$$$$$$
                 /$$__  $$    | $$_____/    | $$__  $$
                | $$  \ $$    | $$          | $$  \ $$
                | $$  | $$    | $$$$$       | $$  | $$
                | $$  | $$    | $$__/       | $$  | $$
                | $$/$$ $$    | $$          | $$  | $$
                |  $$$$$$/ /$$| $$$$$$$$ /$$| $$$$$$$//$$
                 \____ $$$|__/|________/|__/|_______/|__/
                       \__/

13. Printing statistics.

=== solved ===
[...]
   Number of cells:                 12
     $_NOT_                          1
     $and                            6
     $or                             5

=== spec ===
[...]
   Number of cells:                 20
     $_AND_                          2
     $_NOT_                          1
     $and                            9
     $logic_not                      2
     $or                             6
```

Depends:
----
- [x] PR #1996 
- [x] PR #2015 
- [x] PR #2016
- [x] PR #2017 
- [x] PR #2018
- [x] PR #2093 
- [x] PR #2135 
- [x] PR #2138
